title: Quality attributes — check what stuck
intro: Five checks on performance, scaling, and availability.

choices: The system is up 99% of the time
The slowest request ever was 200 ms
questions.explanation: A percentile is about the distribution of response times: p99 = 200 ms means 99% of requests complete within 200 ms. It's a promise about the tail, which the average hides.

questions.prompt: Response time is best understood as…
questions.explanation: Response time = processing + waiting. A fast handler can still be slow if requests queue — often the wait dominates, and you fix it with capacity, not faster code.

questions.prompt: The main advantage of horizontal scaling over vertical scaling is…
choices: It removes the need for a load balancer
It's effectively unbounded and doubles as redundancy
It keeps all state on one machine
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling is capped by the biggest single machine and is a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling adds machines behind a load balancer — effectively unbounded and fault-tolerant — at the cost of statelessness and coordination.
Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). You raise it by failing less often (higher MTBF) or recovering faster (lower MTTR).

choices: A standby takes over on failover; it's idle until then
There is no redundancy at all
Requests are retried on the same node
questions.explanation: Active-passive keeps a standby that takes over when the primary fails — simpler than active-active, but the standby sits idle and failover isn't instant. Active-active has all replicas serving, so a failure just removes capacity.
